title: Isaiah 53
subtitle: The Suffering Servant: Pierced for Our Transgressions

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “By His Wounds We Are Healed”

heb.paragraph: "He was pierced (məḥōlāl) for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed" (v.5). THE most christological chapter in the OT. "Who has believed our message?" (v.1) — John 12:38 and Romans 10:16 quote this. He grew up like a tender shoot, a root out of dry ground (v.2). No beauty, no majesty, nothing to attract us (v.2). Despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering (v.3). "Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering" (v.4) — Matthew 8:17 applies this to Jesus healing the sick. "We all, like sheep, have gone astray, each of us has turned to our own way; and the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all" (v.6). Substitutionary atonement: HIS wounds, OUR healing. HIS punishment, OUR peace.

notes.note: "Who has believed our message? He grew up like a tender shoot, a root out of dry ground." John 12:38; Rom 10:16 quote v.1. The servant is unexpected: no royal splendour, no military power. A root from dry ground — life from death.

"He was despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering, and familiar with pain." The most concise summary of Jesus life in the OT. Despised, rejected, suffering, pain. Four words that define the Passion.

"Pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed." The five-fold substitution: pierced FOR us, crushed FOR us, punished FOR our peace, wounded FOR our healing. The preposition “for” carries the entire theology of the cross.

"We all, like sheep, have gone astray. The LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all." Universal sin ("all have gone astray") matched by universal imputation ("the iniquity of us all"). 1 Peter 2:24–25 quotes this directly.

refs.note: He was in the world, the world did not recognize him; he came to his own, his own did not receive him — the servant's rejection pattern.
Matthew applies 'he took up our infirmities' to Jesus's healing ministry, seeing the servant prophecy fulfilled in compassion.
By his wounds you have been healed; you were like sheep going astray — direct citation of Isaiah 53.
Jesus was delivered for our trespasses and raised for our justification — the servant's substitutionary pattern.

hist.context: Isaiah 53:1–6 is the theological heart of the Hebrew Bible's suffering-for-others theology. The servant grew up like a tender shoot, without beauty or majesty (v.2). He was despised, rejected, a man of suffering (v.3). Yet he bore our griefs and carried our sorrows (v.4). He was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed (v.5). All we like sheep had gone astray, and YHWH laid on him the iniquity of us all (v.6). The substitutionary logic is explicit: our sins, his suffering, our healing.

sections.header: Verses 7–12 — “He Was Led Like a Lamb to the Slaughter”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD makes his life an offering for sin (ʾāshām)" (v.10). "He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth; he was led like a lamb to the slaughter" (v.7) — the Ethiopian eunuch was reading this exact verse when Philip met him (Acts 8:32–35). "By oppression and judgment he was taken away. For the transgression of my people he was punished" (v.8). "He was assigned a grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death" (v.9) — Jesus crucified between criminals, buried in Joseph of Arimathea rich man tomb. "After he has suffered, he will see the light of life and be satisfied" (v.11). The servant DIES and then SEES — resurrection implied. "He bore the sin of many, and made intercession for the transgressors" (v.12). The servant is sacrifice, priest, and intercessor in one.

notes.note: "He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth." Acts 8:32–35: the Ethiopian eunuch reads this and Philip explains Jesus. The lamb-to-slaughter image: Passover lamb (Ex 12), suffering servant (Isa 53), Lamb of God (John 1:29), slain Lamb (Rev 5:6).

"He was assigned a grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death, though he had done no violence, nor was any deceit in his mouth." Wicked in death (crucified between thieves), rich in burial (Joseph of Arimathea). The historical precision is remarkable.

"The LORD makes his life a guilt offering. After he has suffered, he will see the light of life." Death AS offering; life AFTER death. The ʾāshām (guilt offering of Lev 5) applied to a person. Resurrection implied: suffering, then seeing, then satisfaction.

"He poured out his life unto death and was numbered with the transgressors. For he bore the sin of many and made intercession for the transgressors." Luke 22:37: Jesus quotes this at the Last Supper. Numbered with transgressors (crucified between criminals). Bearing sin (2 Cor 5:21). Making intercession (Heb 7:25).

refs.note: The Ethiopian eunuch reads this very passage — sheep to slaughter, silent before shearers — and asks: about whom does the prophet speak?
Some manuscripts cite 'numbered with transgressors' at Jesus's crucifixion between two criminals.
Jesus himself quotes 'numbered with transgressors,' saying it must be fulfilled in him.
Christ was offered once to bear the sins of many — the sacrificial-substitution language drawn directly from Isaiah 53:12.

hist.context: The servant's suffering culminates in death and burial with the wicked, yet with the rich in his death (v.9). YHWH's will was to crush him and cause him to suffer (v.10), yet through his offspring's flourishing and the LORD's purpose prospering, the servant's suffering is vindicated. He will see the light of life after his anguish (v.11). The 'many' language dominates: he bore the sin of many, made many righteous, made intercession for transgressors. The Fourth Servant Song resolves the Servant's identity crisis: this is not Israel (who sins and needs healing) but someone who heals Israel's wound by bearing it himself.

rows.label: The Lamb — 53:7–12
rows.text: "Led like a LAMB to the slaughter." Silent. Grave with wicked, rich in death. "After suffering, he will SEE THE LIGHT OF LIFE." Intercession.

hebtext.note: "He was pierced (məḥōlāl) for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed" (v.5). THE most christological chapter in the OT. "Who has believed our message?" (v.1) — John 12:38 and Romans 10:16 quote this. He grew up like a tender shoot, a root out of dry ground (v.2). No beauty, no majesty, nothing to attract us (v.2). Despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering (v.3). "Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering" (v.4) — Matthew 8:17 applies this to Jesus healing the sick. "We all, like sheep, have gone astray, each of us has turned to our own way; and the LORD has laid on him the iniquity of us all" (v.6). Substitutionary atonement: HIS wounds, OUR healing. HIS punishment, OUR peace.

"The LORD makes his life an offering for sin (ʾāshām)" (v.10). "He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth; he was led like a lamb to the slaughter" (v.7) — the Ethiopian eunuch was reading this exact verse when Philip met him (Acts 8:32–35). "By oppression and judgment he was taken away. For the transgression of my people he was punished" (v.8). "He was assigned a grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death" (v.9) — Jesus crucified between criminals, buried in Joseph of Arimathea rich man tomb. "After he has suffered, he will see the light of life and be satisfied" (v.11). The servant DIES and then SEES — resurrection implied. "He bore the sin of many, and made intercession for the transgressors" (v.12). The servant is sacrifice, priest, and intercessor in one.

themes.note: Covenant, Mercy, AND Prophecy all peak at 10: THE theological climax of the OT. Every NT author quotes or alludes to this chapter. Substitutionary atonement. The lamb. The guilt offering. Resurrection implied. The most important chapter in Isaiah.

coaching.tip: 'By his wounds we are healed' (v. 5). The substitutionary logic is explicit: 'he was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities.' Isaiah doesn't soften this — the Servant's suffering is vicarious, the healing is real, and the mechanism is exchange. The New Testament didn't invent substitutionary atonement; it recognized it in Isaiah 53 and named the Servant.

'He was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent' (v. 7). Philip encounters the Ethiopian eunuch reading this exact verse (Acts 8:32–35) and 'beginning with that Scripture, told him the good news about Jesus.' Isaiah 53 is the hinge between Old and New Testaments — the passage that most directly bridges prophetic expectation and gospel fulfillment. The earliest church read the cross through this chapter.
